coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of nitrogen self-doped carbon dots from rapeseed meal for highly sensitive fluorescence detection of baicalein
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Guoqing
Guo, Ruizhen
Wu, Hui
Ren, Zhilin
Wu, Jun
Zhang, Yi
coredata.dc:description: The rapeseed meal, a type of residual by-product of rapeseed oil production was used as the precursor to prepare nitrogen self-doping carbon dots RM-CDs through an easy hydrothermal process. Thanks to the introduction of nitrogen element and oxygen-containing functional groups, RM-CDs had a fluorescence quantum yield of 18.6 %. The RM-CDs demonstrated highly stable to the ionic strength, pH, and ultraviolet radiation. However, their bright blue fluorescence was significantly weakened by baicalein owing to the inner-filter effect and static quenching effect. As a result, RM-CDs could be used as the fluorescent sensing materials for baicalein detection with a wide range of linear response (0.5–170 μM) as well as low limit of detection (0.046 μM). Moreover, the as-developed sensing platform was also applicable to detect baicalein in actual samples. The developed RM-CDs was sustainably prepared from the biomass by-product of rapeseed meal, this approach expanded the application scopes of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rapeseed meal
Carbon dots
Baicalein
Fluorescence quench

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of RM-CDs from rapeseed meal
Proposed sensing mechanism of RM-CDs for baicalein
Detection of baicalein in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM image (A), particle size distribution (B) and FT-IR spectrum (C) of RM-CDs.
(A) The survey XPS spectra of RM-CDs; the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (B), O 1s (C), N 1s (D).
(A) The UV–vis, excitation and emission spectra of RM-CDs (inset: the picture under sunlight and UV light); (B) the emission spectra under different excitation wavelength.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of detection system (inset: the picture of detection system without (a) and with (b) baicalein); (B) The linear fit curve between ln(F0/F) value and baicalein concentration (0.5–170 μM).

(A) Selectivity of the RM-CDs for baicalein over controls; (B) Interference of the RM-CDs for baicalein (the concentrations of controls were 300 μM, the concentration of baicalein was 150 μM); (C) The photographs of RM-CDs in the presence of controls under UV lamp.

(A) the UV–vis absorption spectrum of baicalein, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs; (B) the fluorescence lifetime spectra of N-CDs with and without addition of baicalein; (C) the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, baicalein and N-CDs + baicalein; (D) the linear relationship between F0/F value and the concentrations of baicalein (0–50 μM).
Comparison of different analytical methods for baicalein measurement.
The baicalein measurement in real samples.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of nitrogen self-doped carbon dots from rapeseed meal for highly sensitive fluorescence detection of baicalein

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Hubei University of Arts and Science, Xiangyang 441053, Hubei Province, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Longzhong Laboratory, Xiangyang 441000, Hubei, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemical Engineering, Hubei University of Arts and Science, Xiangyang 441053, Hubei Province, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei University of Arts and Science

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapeseed meal, a type of residual by-product of rapeseed oil production was used as the precursor to prepare nitrogen self-doping carbon dots RM-CDs through an easy hydrothermal process. Thanks to the introduction of nitrogen element and oxygen-containing functional groups, RM-CDs had a fluorescence quantum yield of 18.6 %. The RM-CDs demonstrated highly stable to the ionic strength, pH, and ultraviolet radiation. However, their bright blue fluorescence was significantly weakened by baicalein owing to the inner-filter effect and static quenching effect. As a result, RM-CDs could be used as the fluorescent sensing materials for baicalein detection with a wide range of linear response (0.5–170 μM) as well as low limit of detection (0.046 μM). Moreover, the as-developed sensing platform was also applicable to detect baicalein in actual samples. The developed RM-CDs was sustainably prepared from the biomass by-product of rapeseed meal, this approach expanded the application scopes of CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Baicalein (Fig. S1) is a flavonoid compound extracted from the dried roots of Scutellaria baicalensis [1,2]. In recent years, numerous experiments have proved that baicalein has the extensive biological and pharmacological activities in antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, anti-apoptotic, and improving learning and memory abilities [3–5]. It has a wide range of functions, with multiple sites of action and complex and diverse mechanisms of action. In addition, some studies have found that baicalein is expected to become a safe, inexpensive, and broad-spectrum effective anti-tumor drug as well as a potential ideal drug for treating Alzheimer's disease [6–8]. However, an unconscionable intake of baicalein can have many side effects such as spasms, blurriness, and dizziness on the human body [9]. Thus, developing applicable methods to measure the baicalein contents of the human body is extremely important. Until now, diverse technologies have been attempted to measure baicalein, for example high performance liquid chromatography/mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [10], thin layer chromatography (TLC) [11,12], voltammetry [13,14], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [15], electrochemical methods [16–18] and UV–vis spectrophotometry [19]. However, some ineluctable drawbacks such as time-consuming, high cost, clumsy operation, and low sensitivity hinder their convenient application in samples analysis [20,21]. Thus, seeking an easy, fast, and highly sensitive means for the detection of baicalein in real samples is of great significance.

In recent years, fluorescence methods have drawn great concern considering their high accuracy, simple operation, low price, and wide applicability [22,23]. Some fluorescent materials such as copper nanoclusters [24], phosphorus modified graphitic C3N4 nanomaterials [25], and nitrogen and sulfur dual-doped carbon quantum dots [26] have been developed for the determination of baicalein. Among them, CDs have received more and more concerns because of their lots of advantages, such as simplicity of preparation, low-toxic, good biocompatibility, high water solubility and excellent optical properties [27–29]. Usually, carbon containing organic small molecules such as citric acid are considered as raw materials for hydrothermal preparing of CDs. For improving their fluorescence performance of CDs, doping with additional elements like P, N, and S is considered an effective method [30,31]. Zhang et al. [26] prepared the N,S-doped CDs from citric acid and N-acetyl-L-cysteine with a high quantum yield of 49 %, and the synthesized NS-CDs were successfully applied to the measurement of baicalein with a LOD of 0.21 μM in the linear range from 0.69 to 70.0 μM. Recently, our groups synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots from p-phenylenediamine and ascorbic acid with good fluorescence stability, and presented a satisfactory linear curve for diosmetin within the limits of 0.5–150 μM [32]. Furthermore, the pyrolysis of heteroatoms-containing precursor materials can also obtain the heteroatoms doped CDs. Yin et al. [33] prepared N-CDs with improved the luminescence emission by carbonization of nitrogen and carbon-rich precursors such as 3-(3,4-dihydroxy phenyl)-L-alanine, L-histidine, and L-arginine. Wang et al. [25] developed a phosphorus modified C3N4) nanomaterial by pyrolysis of P-g-C3N4 for detection of baicalein within 2.0–30 μM. However, considering the limitations such as cost and environmental factors of chemical preparation of carbon dots, a green and renewable method for the development of CDs from the natural source seems to be a superior choice to meet the need of sustainability [34–39]. Rapeseed meal is an abundant and low-cost by-product derived from the mass production of rapeseed oil. It contains the components of protein (about 35 %), crude fiber, and carbohydrates [40]. It is reported that the rapeseed meal is usually used as organic fertilizer and feed for agricultural production and animal husbandry [41,42], with low utilization value. However, to our knowledge, there is no study on utilizing the rapeseed meal to produce fluorescent materials for the sensing and detection. Given that rapeseed meal is rich in nitrogen elements in protein, and it is entirely possible to serve as a nitrogen-rich bio-based raw material for synthesizing nitrogen doped carbon dots. Moreover, pressing treatment of rapeseed meal is beneficial for improving the efficiency of hydrothermal reaction, and improves the yield of carbon dots.

Herein, a sensitive nitrogen self-doped carbon dots (RM-CDs) probe was developed through an effective hydrothermal method using rapeseed meal, a type of residual by-product of rapeseed oil production as the precursor without additional addition of nitrogen-containing materials (Scheme S1). The possible mechanisms for RM-CDs preparation have been discussed, and the correlation between the structure characteristics and their optical properties was surveyed. The fluorescence of synthesized RM-CDs was visibly weakened by baicalein based on the inner filter effect and static quenching. Consequently, the baicalein concentration was determined through the linear experiments. The as-developed carbon dots were also successfully used in measuring of baicalein in actual samples.

The rapeseed meal (Xiangyang, China) employed in present research was sourced from a local oil processing plant, and the level of N in the rapeseed meal was calculated to be 8.28 % according to the results of ultimate analysis. AgNO3, KCl, LiCl, PdCl2, CdCl2, NaCO3, KH2PO4, K2HPO4, D-alanine (D-Ala), DL-phenylalanine (DL-Phe), D-serine (D-Ser), D-histidine (D-His), D-threonine (D-Thr), D-proline (D-Pro), valine (Val) and baicalein (Bai) were obtained from Macklin without any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of RM-CDs from rapeseed meal
ce:section.ce:para: The rapeseed meal was washed by deionized (DI) water and methanol for three times, then dried at 100 °C overnight, subsequently ground into powder by a crusher. The 2.0 g of rapeseed meal power was mixed with 50 mL deionized water, and poured the mixture into a 100 mL steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated for 2 h at 200 °C. After hydrothermal reaction, the dark-brownish color solution and solid biochar were collected by filter. The collected yellowish-brown solution was dialyzed via a dialysis bag, then the rapeseed meal derived carbon dots powders were attained by freeze-drying dialysate, denoted as RM-CDs. The obtained solid biochar was used by us to prepare soil remediation agents and catalytic materials.

The fluorescence spectra of the RM-CDs were recorded by an F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with the excitation/emission slits of 5 nm/5 nm. The fluorescence decay curves were tested on an FELIX32 Fluorescence Lifetime spectrometer (Photon Technology International, USA). The UV–vis spectra were recorded through a UV-2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with a 1 cm × 1 cm UV cuvette. The morphology and sizes of the RM-CDs were tested by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100). An IR PRESTIGE-21 spectrometer was used to obtain the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of N-CDs in the range of 500–4000 cm−1. The surface chemistry of RM-CDs was examined by a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi + X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, using a monochrome Al target, and the binding energy was corrected by C 1 s.

The determination of fluorescence quantum yield referred to the previous literature [43]. The quinine sulfate (a quantum yield of 54 % in the presence of 0.1 M H2SO4) was selected as a standard substance. The quantum yield of RM-CDs was determined by a formula as below:
(1)
Q =
Q S
×
A S
/
A X
×
I X
/
I S
×
η X
/
η S
2
where Q is the quantum yield, A is the UV absorbance, I refers to the fluorescence value, η is the solvent refraction index. The subscripts S and X stand for quinine sulfate and RM-CDs, respectively. According to the calculation results, the Q value of the RM-CDs was 18.6 %.

A buffer solution with a pH of 6 was used to dilute the RM-CDs. Various baicalein amounts were added into the RM-CDs solution and fully interacted for 1.0 min under ambient condition. Subsequently the fluorescence spectrum of the mixing solutions was determined at excitation wavelength 365 nm. F0 represents the fluorescence value of mixture without baicalein, and F refers to the fluorescence value of sample with baicalein. Linear fitting was conducted based on the fluorescence results of ln(F0/F) and the baicalein concentrations to establish the standard curve for sensing baicalein.

To prove the feasibleness of this sensor for baicalein determination in a real-life sample, the baicalein concentrations in the samples of glucose solution and bovine serum were calculated according to the obtained standard curve.

The structure properties of as-prepared RM-CDs were investigated by TEM, FT-IR and XPS. The morphological characterization of RM-CDs was examined by TEM as presented in Fig. 1 (A), the picture displayed that the RM-CDs are the sphericity with high dispersion, which were similar to other CDs reported in the literature [44]. From Fig. 1(B) of particles distribution of RM-CDs, the size ranged between 3 and 5 nm and an average nano-particle size was 4.25 ± 0.84 nm. The surface functional groups of carbon dots were researched by FT-IR technology as presented in Fig. 1(C). The band located at ca. 3450 cm−1 was found, which was corresponded to the characteristic vibration of O–H and/or N–H bond. A weak peak at 2969 cm−1 was connected with the presence of C–H. The stretching vibrations centering at 1591 cm−1 as well as 1059 cm−1 could be related to the existence of CO/CC and C-O bands, respectively. Moreover, the obvious peaks located at 1350–1450 cm−1 appeared, which was ascribed to C-N bands [31,32,38]. The above-mentioned infrared results revealed the introduction of lots of hydrophilic groups including hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl and amino groups on RM-CDs surface, which was not only beneficial for improving their quantum yield, but also for the dispersion of carbon dots in water to detect target substances [35].

The element component and chemical information of RM-CDs were further investigated by XPS analysis. From the survey XPS spectrum in Fig. 2 (A), the distinct peaks located at ca. 531.2 eV (21.5 %), 399.4 eV (25.3 %) and 284.5 eV (53.3 %) were clearly observed, which ascribed to the binding energy of O 1 s, N 1 s, and C 1 s, respectively. The results confirmed the existence of C, N, and O elements in RM-CDs, which was consistent with the discoveries of FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 2(B), C1s high-resolution spectrum could be resolved to three peaks, i.e. 284.7 eV, 286.1 eV and 288.1 eV, which were deduced to be connected with the presence of CC, C–N and CO, respectively. For O 1s spectrum in Fig. 2 (C), it was divided into the peaks centered at 530.3, 531.5, and 532.6 eV, they were associated with O–C–O, C–O–H, and CO, respectively. As displayed in Fig. 2 (D), three clear deconvolution peaks around 398.7 eV, 400.2 eV and 401.3 eV were identified for N 1s spectrum, which could be related with C-N, SP3 hybrid N-(C)3 and N–H bonds, respectively [32,45].

The formation of C-N bonds as well as other hydrophilic functional groups in the carbon dots framework may be closely related to the hydrothermal processes in a high temperature and pressure. A possible formation mechanism of RM-CDs from rapeseed meal via a one-pot hydrothermal method was illustrated in Scheme S1. Rapeseed meal is well known to be composed of protein, fiber, and carbohydrates. After harsh mechanical pressing, the internal structure of rapeseed meal is partially destroyed, which is beneficial for its cleavage of C-O-C and C-N in peptide bond into small active molecules such as amino acids and aromatic compounds with oxygen-containing groups during high-temperature hydrothermal processes [31]. Subsequently, these active molecules underwent polymerization during the hydrothermal process to form small-sized nanoparticles. Meanwhile, aromatic reforming happened to promote sp2 hybridization. As a result, nitrogen contents were smoothly introduced into the carbon skeleton, and the RM-CDs surface still retained some oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups, affording them outstanding fluorescence properties.

The optical features of rapeseed meal derived RM-CDs were researched based on the results of the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As presented in Fig. 3 (A), a clear peak located around 275 nm was identified, which was likely corresponded to the π-π * transition of aromatic sp2 carbon and surface groups [46]. Further research of the fluorescence characteristics of RM-CDs confirmed their optimal excitation (Ex) wavelength of 365 nm and emission (Em) wavelength of 445 nm. From the image inside Fig. 3(A), the prepared RM-CDs solution displayed yellow brown under visible sunlight, while intriguingly having a bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation at 365 nm. Moreover, the fluorescent emission features of RM-CDs were investigated by changing the excitation wavelengths from 335 to 395 nm. As presented in Fig. 3(B), a significant excitation-dependent performance was found for fluorescence spectra. As increasing the excitation wavelength, the fluorescence emission intensities followed a tendency of increasing first and diminishing later. It is well known that the generation of fluorescence requires excitation light, and the number of electrons undergoing transitions varies at different excitation wavelengths, resulting in different fluorescence emission intensities [47]. Moreover, an apparent red shift of fluorescence emission peak occurred with the increase in excitation wavelengths. The above optical behavior of RM-CDs could be due to their particle size, and the presence of various surface emissive states that resulted from functional groups [38,47].

The fluorescence stability of nanomaterial was a key factor in practical applications for sensing systems. Therefore, the effects of various conditions including storage time, ionic strengths, UV exposure, and environmental pH on the values of RM-CDs fluorescence were studied. From Fig. S2, the RM-CDs showed a high stability within 10 days with only slight reduction of fluorescence value at both 4 ℃ and 25 ℃. From Fig. S3, the RM-CDs only showed a negligible decline in fluorescence intensity after 5 min of irradiation under 365 nm UV light, indicating their excellent photostability. Moreover, the influence of ionic strengths on fluorescence of RM-CDs was surveyed by means of mixing with NaCl solution of concentrations varying from 0-0.5 M. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence values of RM-CDs remained almost unchanged even the NaCl concentration reached to 0.5 M. Finally, the effects of pH values on RM-CDs fluorescence were examined as presented in Fig. S5. The values of fluorescence maintained almost unchanged between pH 6 to 8, but decreased significantly under over-acidic (pH less than 5) and over-basic (pH upwards 11) conditions. The obvious weaken in the fluorescence intensity in over-acidic and over-basic conditions was related to the protonation of amino groups under over-acidic media and deprotonation of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups under over-basic media [36,48]. These results demonstrate that the present rapeseed meal derived carbon dots showed good stability under complicated conditions, which was helpful for its practical application in sensing fields.

A novel fluorescence sensing nanomaterials, RM-CDs, were employed to measure baicalein contens. The fluorescence quenching experiments for RM-CDs were conducted by adding of diverse baicalein concentrations (0.5–170 µM) at the optimal conditions of detection time 1.0 min and pH value 6.0, respectively, and the recorded fluorescence spectra are displayed in Fig. 4 (A). It was found that the fluorescent emission intensities of RM-CDs at 445 nm progressively diminished with raising the baicalein concentration. As the baicalein concentrations increased to 170 μM, the fluorescence quenching efficiency reached up to 95 %, and correspondingly, the fluorescence image showed that the blue fluorescence of RM-CDs almost disappeared under UV lamp irradiation. From Fig. 4(B), it was found that a good linear relationship was established on the basis of the connection among ln(F0/F) and baicalein concentrations of 0.5–170 μM, and the linear formula was ln(F0/F) = 0.0170[Q]-0.00078 with R2 of 0.9987. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.046 μM based on the reported calculation formula LOD = 3b/k (b referred to the standard deviation of the blank solution, and k represented the slope of the fitting curve between the F0/F value and baicalein concentrations) [32]. Furthermore, the determination performances of other reported sensing methods were compared with the present sensor based on the RM-CDs from rapeseed meal. From Table 1 , significantly, the as-prepared RM-CDs exhibited an obviously wider range of linearity with low detection limit. A superior detection performance, inexpensive preparation raw material, and simple preparation processes make it show great potential for application in detecting baicalein.

To employ the sensor to determine baicalein in real samples, a number of potential interferences, including metal ions and non-metal ions (K+, CO3 2–, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, etc.), as well as some amino acids (D-Ala, DL-Phe, D-Ser, etc.), were applied to judge the selectivity of as-developed RM-CDs for sensing baicalein. From Fig. 5 (A), all investigated substances except baicalein cannot significantly change the fluorescence intensity of RM-CDs, revealing their high selectivity to baicalein. Furthermore, the anti-interference experiments were also conducted by measuring the fluorescence values of RM-CDs when the baicalein and interfering substances coexist. Fig. 5(B) suggested that the interfering substances showed little impact on the determination of baicalein using present RM-CDs as sensors. Additionally, the images of RM-CDs with various investigated interfering substances and baicalein under UV light are presented in Fig. 5(C), we found that only baicalein could effectively weaken their fluorescence. In brief, the newly developed nitrogen self-doped carbon dots derived from sustainable rapeseed meal has high selectivity as well as anti-interference ability in the fluorescence measurement of baicalein.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed sensing mechanism of RM-CDs for baicalein
ce:section.ce:para: The possible reason of obvious quenching effect of baicalein on RM-CDs was discussed by systematically investigating the optical features of RM-CDs, baicalein, and their interrelationship. As displayed in Fig. 6 (A), it was observed that there was a clear overlapping wavelength region among the UV–vis absorption spectrum of baicalein and the excitation spectrum of RM-CDs, which indicated that the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) could be the underlying cause of fluorescence quenching [49,50]. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetimes of RM-CDs with baicalein and without baicalein were tested as displayed in Fig. 6(B). The calculated results exhibited that an average lifetime of RM-CDs with baicalein was 5.05 ns, which was very similar to that without baicalein (5.09 ns). The results of almost unchanged fluorescence lifetimes indicated that the FRET effect was not the reason for fluorescence quenching, and also suggested that the static quenching rather than dynamic quenching might be another major reason for its fluorescence quenching. It was reported that the static quenching effect is resulted from the generation of new non-fluorescen substances through coordination of fluorescent substance and the quencher [51]. To prove the above speculation, the interrelationship of RM-CDs and baicalein should be revealed by the mean of UV–vis spectroscopy. From Fig. 6(C), an absorption peak at 275 nm disappeared for RM-CDs, but additional peaks at 256.82 nm and 358.05 nm were identified for N-CDs + baicalein, which suggested that the generation of new non-fluorescent groups, leading to the static quenching effect.

Furthermore, the static quenching was also verified through the Stern-Volmer equation (F0/F = 1 + KSV[Q] = 1 + Kqτ0[Q]) [50,52]. According to the fitted linear curve shown in Fig. 6(D), the Stern-Volmer quenching constant (KSV) has been calculated as 2.7 × 104 L mol−1, and the bimolecular quenching constant (Kq) was 5. 3 × 1012 L mol−1 s−1, whcih was much higher than the maximum scattering collision quenching constant (2 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1), confirming static quenching rather than dynamic quenching was another reason of fluorescence quenching [50]. In conclusion, the IFE and static quenching were the main reasons of sensing mechanism of RM-CDs for baicalein.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of baicalein in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For studying the reliability of as-developed RM-CDs sensor in measuring baicalein in real samples, the baicalein content in glucose and bovine serum samples was measured by using a spiked method. As various concentrations of baicalein (20, 40 and 60 μM) were mixed with the glucose and bovine serum samples, the measurement of baicalein concentration was performed on the basis of the changes of fluorescence intensity for RM-CDs based on the obtained linear formula (as shown in Fig. 4(B)). According to the results in Table 2 , the as-prepared sensing materials showed favorable average recovery of 98.1–104.8 %, and high reproducibility and accuracy with RSD of 1.23–3.27 %. These results suggested that RM-CDs derived from rapeseed meal had good potential applications in sensing baicalein in real samples.

In present work, an easy, cost-effective and eco-friendly approach was designed for the preparation of nitrogen self-doped carbon dots RM-CDs from rapeseed meal, a type of residual by-product of rapeseed oil production. The as-prepared RM-CDs showed the typical spherical nano-particles and had plentiful functional groups, which made it useful for fluorescence determination of baicalein based on the fluorescent repression. The determination linear range of baicalein was as wide as 0.5–170 μM with a low LOD of 0.046 μM, which was better than the results reported in most literature. The IFE and static quenching effects were considered to be the proposed reasons of fluorescent quenching of RM-CDs by baicalein. The developed sensing platform also showed high selectivity and inertness to the interference of different ions and amino acids. Moreover, it could be successfully used for baicalein detection in glucose and bovine serum sample. In conclusion, the RM-CDs show a big potential to act as good fluorescent sensors for chemical sensing.

Guoqing Deng: Investigation, Methodology. Ruizhen Guo: Conceptualization. Hui Wu: Characterization analysis, Writing – review & editing. Zhilin Ren: Software, Validation. Jun Wu: Data acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Yi Zhang: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Hubei Natural Science Foundation Innovation and Development Joint Fund Project (2022CFD099), the Student Innovation Training Program (X202410519102) for financial support, supported by Hubei Key Laboratory of Low Dimensional Optoelectronic Material and Devices (HLOM242017).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125672.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Green Chem. Lett. Rev.

J. Funct. Foods
